item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The images used in this article were made for the article, and thus are cc-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh my God.

YES

item.title: WTF, that's a good SCP
item.content:encoded: How you explained SCP-2311 is outstanding, for it abilities in a rage to even its origin makes this SCP not just good, but even emotional

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: When will we be able to upvote comments.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I didn't get much of this stuff at all, but I think I understand a bit better now. Thanks.

Still, I feel an SCP should stand on its own and not require me to look up the comments…

Regardless, it was rather creepy and interesting. So no vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Damn it I knew this! Even managed to call it "heliophobia" instead of "photophobia" for similar reasons. Thanks for the catch; this has been corrected.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "Hydrophobia" is commonly understood to mean rabies, not fear of water. To avoid ambiguity, "aquaphobia" is unambiguously understood to mean fear of water.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A somewhat unique spin on a standard SCP formula, executed… Well, perfectly. It's not too often I read an article and come away thinking "that could not have been done better."

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thinking about it more, I think he intended to use the Plastic restraints on his dates… you know, the dates he never got.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Doesn't that depend on the coefficient of heat transfer as much as the temperature?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
DAMN, this one was… Quite disturbing. And good. Only one problem I can think of:

the extreme cold of this liquid did affect human tissue as expected, inflicting nerve damage after 5 minutes of continuous exposure

A liquid at -200 celcius would do that damage much, much faster. It's really cold at -200°C.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The plastic restraints were unopened because plastic is scientific and magicbreaking.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
WOW.

…

+ show block
– hide block

…

My thanks to Abettik who helped clarify a few things I wasn't sure about…

…

Yup. Or, more specifically, using a homunculus to conduct The Forbidden Experiment in hopes of regaining the "language of Adam."

You mean, POI 'just' wanted Adam's language and created a reality-bender without knowing?

…

my own crappy childhood

………. 8'-(

I'm speechless.

This jumps aaaaaaaall the way to the top - somewhere in my top 10, or even 5 favourite skips.

ETA: reminded of this interesting, albeit harsh, experiment:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=apzXGEbZht0

EXCELLENT writing, DisquietRiot!
<me hug you, for several reasons>

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fascinating. I recall an anecdote of a Mughal emperor who tried to find out the "language of Adam" by kidnapping babies once.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Kinda with Jacob Conwell on this, and a neutral vote.

I feel like the creator character is pretty over-the-top. It's like you were going for someone who embodied all of humanity's most monstrous habits in search of power. There's human trafficking (I assume), forced marriage, spousal rape, human sacrifice, child abuse, and to top it all off, a flippant comment about horse sex. The homunculus is a little better, but then he also reminded me of the mentally challenged kid with super healing powers in Series I.

It's not a bad article, but it's got a combination of the ridiculous and the cliched. If there is one thing I liked, though, it's the explanation for how he got the septal defect, that was a nice detail.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So +1, even though, once again, it's another scip without glowing rabid monkeys.

Thanks! Though, I mean… I could always throw some in I guess? Not sure what you're referencing. Care to elucidate?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nowhere near.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not sure what is worse… the unsettling origin story, or the fact that it actually seemed to work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ever-burning orangutans aren't close enough?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nice tone, plausible containment, but… The "found diary/log which explains all" seems to be getting old, but… I liked it overall but…. It's a reality bending widdle kid but… It was done deftly.

So +1, even though, once again, it's another scip without glowing rabid monkeys.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For me this works where it could have failed because it doesn't perpetuate "reality bending" as a generic power source, unlike a lot of "magical child" (and magical humanoids in general) seem to do. Which I find to be both lazy writing, and a bad fit for wainscot horror in general (generic power sources and source specific nullifiers are better elements for a superhero setting, IMO). This has an origin which is all of unique, horrible, and difficult to replicate, which is the ideal kind of origin for this setting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Agreeing with Jacob Conwell. The description was decent but the addendum really drove this article home.

Nice job dude.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For what it's worth, I know "spooky magic kid" is a cliche and that "poor widdle reality bender" is a trope so popular on this site that it's rapidly approaching cliche. I was close to abandoning this project early on for those reasons, but people seemed to like the manuscript enough that I was encouraged to go for it. But I'll admit this piece is leaning very hard on the manuscript, which is why I spent a lot of time and effort on that section in particular. I wrote most of the prop on the subway and probably creeped some commuters out with my arts and crafts time, heh.

I'm of the opinion that solid execution can overcome the fact that an idea has been done before. I'm glad all the effort I put into "daddy's" manuscript at least saved me from your down vote. I knew going in this wouldn't be everyone's cup of tea, so I'm going to round this up to a win.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yup. Or, more specifically, using a homunculus to conduct The Forbidden Experiment in hopes of regaining the "language of Adam."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Glad I'm finally livin' up to the name.

EDIT: In response to your edit, you pretty much nailed it and picked up on all the squicky details. For the one thing you didn't get, I'll refer you to TL333s' post on the next page.

Death of the Author
ZOMG ZOMBIE AUTHOR

"The waters" = amniotic fluid and "drove the heart from the center" = "gave him a ventricular septal defect."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Alright, so… this was a thing.

The description of this article did nothing to really wow me. It just came across as a rather run of the mill reality bender, albeit a highly damaged one at that. Additionally, the list of items just came across as a mixture of spooky effects jumbled together for effect. The recovery log, likewise, didn't do much for me which is a shame as Psi-7 is a pretty neat MTF.

I did enjoy the journal entries. They were creepy, and did draw me in. To be honest, had you used the journal entries as a stand alone tale I would have upvoted that in a heart beat.

I'm going to no vote for now. To me the description is bland, but that last addenda is pretty cool.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was wondering the other night when this would show up. Bloody fantastic.

item.title: Minor nitpicks
item.content:encoded:
Overall, I like it, but I have some reservations about the tone.

"Medically impossible rate" doesn't really make sense to me; "impossible rate" alone would be fine, but "medically impossible" doesn't make sense when you're describing a disease's effects, as medicine is the scientific study of healing, not hurting. "Medically impossible" would make more sense if you were describing, say, the red pills that *heal* in ways that are impossible for our medical capabilities to replicate. Further, anomalously fast necrosis would be impossible regardless of the existence of medicine whatsoever.

I don't like "sat-site". I really don't like it. See, this document is supposed to serve the purpose of secure, contain, and protect, and usually I wouldn't start shortening phrases in such a safety document (that's what it is, really), as I don't want even the dumbest reader to make any mistakes. I could totally imagine a newbie agent thinking that the contracted name and the full name of the site are two distinct areas.

You should have footnotes explaining the gist of the diseases you mention in the start of the description. Again, this is an instructional safety document, and should be written in such a way that agents don't have to go elsewhere to interpret the document.

Overall, though, not bad. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So it's like a homunculus or a recreation of the First Man (the symbolic 'Adam').

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was initially skeptical about another loose-cannon kid reality bender, but this article's highlight is obviously the recovered notebook, which I think you simply nailed, language- and tone-wise. I could see this one trickling into Keter territory, but it appears that they have a pretty good handle on him.

for now

+1!

Rand-o thoughts:
- I really would love to hear what kind of conversation PoI-2311-01 and SCP-2610-A would get in to.

- I hope personnel "who have survived at least one reality restructuring event" receive official Foundation-wear that reads:

I MADE IT THROUGH A LEVEL-5 ONTOCLYSM
AND ALL I GOT WAS THIS LOUSY T-SHIRT

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"It teaches me the original language of Man or else it gets the hose again!"

Man, this one's creepy. Poor kid. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

Started out mundane enough but got very creepy and emotional, especially in the diary. I'm a little disquieted right now. I'll be thinking about it for a little bit.

EDIT:

+ Show
- Hide

So I have indeed been thinking about this one on and off all day… there's so many little details that fit together, so many phrases that are caught in my head.

Manifest and attempt to consume approximately 20 kilograms of La Perruche brand individually-wrapped sugar cubes.

It's very sad that this poor creature's idea of a good time, like the best thing it can think to do with its powers is to manifest sugar cubes. Obviously because that's the only treat it was given growing up. Nice, unsettling touch.

In one recorded incident, SCP-2311 emitted a stream of liquid at an estimated pressure of

…and one of his only known defenses is to spray people with extremely cold water… an anomalous recreation of the firehose he's known all his life. Another nice, unsettling touch.

two unopened boxes of Rothco-brand plastic restraints.

I've yet to figure out why they're unopened.

but the deceased appears to have undergone a botched hysterectomy.

Right, because of the mess later on where father rips out her womb and makes a weirdo cocoon out of it before waiting for it to "decant."

A bassinet, a crib, and a twin-sized mattress sans bedding, all badly soiled.

No bedding, all badly soiled. Yuck.

Started searching for suitable source material. Rusty.

Nice way to say he can't get a date.

Source material arrived today. Made it official at the courthouse.

Well at least he married her.

Not really all it's cracked up to be.

Nice way to show he's a virgin before this.

Failing health of source material a concern.

Urgh. Didn't catch this on my first readthrough. So he's probably got her chained up in the basement and not taking good care of her, and raping her.

The womb is simply that primordial void of so many creation myths. A vacuum paradoxically containing all necessities for creation.

A really good line… an fascinating idea that breaks out of creep for a second, then dives right back in.

Blurred gender as much as I could without risking critical defect; masculine + feminine, sun + moon, yin + yang, all that good shit.

Great bits in here which explain the deformities.

Alterations continue. Parted the waters and drove the heart from the center.

I still don't know what this means, but I don't think it's good. Is this the hysterectomy?

Vessel spent and disposed of. Think I earned a fucking drink.

Just… just awful. He killed her in a horrible, inhumane way without a second thought and all he can think is, "All in a day's work."

nitrohydrochloric acid

I only just realized why he keeps bringing this up. This is man's name for the chemical, and because he can't "unlearn" this name, he can't get it to work magically.

Well done!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My second SCP, based on a combination of Paul Auster's City of Glass, a messed-up nightmare I had, and my own crappy childhood. Glad to put this one out there, I've been working on it entirely too long. The images still turned out wonky; if any Wikidot wizard wants to touch that up, I'd be most appreciative, but right now I have to run to work.

Big thank you to those of you that looked at this in chat or in the forums.
